project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase access to lower cost and cleaner electricity supply in project areas and to reestablish the electricity supply industry
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1.1 million households (7 million people including 3.5 million females), 585 health and education facilities (205 health facilities, 380 schools), electricity service providers (ESPs), and sector institutions

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on major load centers
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Somalia is a fragile and conflict-affected state. The project operates in areas affected by ongoing conflict, particularly in south and central Somalia. Security remains a challenge despite political stabilization efforts. The project includes security risk management frameworks and contingency plans for restricted site access.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Subtransmission and Distribution Network Reconstruction, Reinforcement and Operations Efficiency
key_components.description: Improve network reliability and operational efficiency in Mogadishu and Hargeisa through: (1a) Generator synchronization and automation to reduce wet stacking and improve efficiency; (1b) Sub-transmission and distribution network interconnection to enable generation synchronization and reduce duplication. Includes 132kV sub-transmission lines, 33/11/LV distribution lines, and distribution equipment.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Hybridization and Battery Storage Systems for Minigrids
key_components.description: Support installation of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) systems at existing diesel-based generation stations in selected load centers. Aims to optimize generation capacity, reduce diesel consumption, and lower tariffs. Selection based on regional balance, maximum impact, optimized costs, land availability, and ESP willingness to maintain performance standards.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Stand-alone Solar Off-grid Access to Public Institutions
key_components.description: Provide electricity access to 585 priority health and education facilities (205 health facilities including hospitals and maternal health clinics; 380 schools including primary, secondary, tertiary, and non-formal education). Includes design, supply, installation, and 5-year operation and maintenance of Lighting Global-certified SPV systems. Prioritizes rural/remote areas, maternal health centers, secondary schools, and areas with IDPs and high poverty.

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Institutional Development and Capacity Building
key_components.description: Five sub-components: (4.1) Policy and regulatory development for sector governance; (4.2) Sector planning and feasibility studies for renewable energy; (4.3) ESP and ministry business support services; (4.4) Project implementation support including E&S safeguards; (4.5) Gender action plan implementation. Supports reestablishment of electricity supply industry institutions with clear roles and responsibilities.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Energy and Water Resources (MoEWR) for Federal Government of Somalia; Ministry of Energy and Minerals (MoEM) for Somaliland; in coordination with Federal Member States, Ministries of Health and Education, and Electricity Service Providers (ESPs)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) at FGS and Somaliland levels for overall oversight and policy guidance; Energy Sector Working Group (ESWG) at MoEWR/MoEM level for sector dialogue and coordination; Project Implementation Units (PIUs) at both FGS and Somaliland with dedicated staff for procurement, financial management, safeguards, and M&E
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (November 2020); Prior review of all contracts; Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents; STEP portal for tracking; Hands-on Enhanced Implementation Support (HEIS) for high-risk/high-value procurements; Owner's Engineer (OE) for design, procurement, and contract management support; Business Support Services Firm (BSSF) to support ESPs
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PIUs responsible for M&E system with PDO and intermediate indicators; Quarterly and annual progress reports; Independent monitoring and verification firm for technical, fiduciary, and safeguards audits; Mid-term review and end-of-term review; Sex-disaggregated data collection; Beneficiary satisfaction surveys; Database for each component to track implementation progress

identified_risks.risk_description: Relations among FGS, FMS, and Somaliland could impact implementation. Current uncertainty regarding FGS elections could heighten tensions and shift development priorities. Lack of FGS-GoSL agreement for IDA funds flow is a risk. Potential contestation around project amounts and locations.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close coordination with FGS, FMS, GoSL, and ESP agencies; Activities prioritized per Power Master Plan and geospatial analysis; Ongoing discussions between World Bank, FGS, and GoSL to establish IDA funds flow arrangements

identified_risks.risk_description: Sector operations mainly financed by private ESPs. COVID-19 impact on ESP operations including reduced demand, nonpayment of utility bills, and impact on cash flows and sustainability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design includes capacity building and business support services to ESPs; Technical assistance for improved sector planning and operations; Vaccination rollout expected to enable resumption of activities

identified_risks.risk_description: Low capacity within implementing agencies (ministries of energy) and ESPs risks undermining timely and effective project implementation. Lack of adequate capacity for implementation, design, and contracts management.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of existing PIUs from ongoing SEAP project; Owner's Engineer/supervision consultant to support PIUs; Capacity-building activities under Component 4; Technical assistance for sector institutions; Strengthened budget allocation for O&M post-project

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management and procurement environment in Somalia remains challenging with potential mismanagement, fraud, lack of transparency, and corruption. Weak institutional and systems capacities at federal, state, and regional levels.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of country systems (UCS) with technical assistance and knowledge transfer; External Assistance Fiduciary Section (EAFS) units for FM oversight; Dedicated FM and procurement specialists in PIUs; On-the-job training; Periodic World Bank reviews; Annual external audits; Strong internal controls and procedures; Disclosure of procurement information

identified_risks.risk_description: High complexity of activities with potential impacts including: management of ESP generation facilities; disposal of hazardous wastes (PCBs, transformer oils, heavy metals, e-waste); soil erosion; fauna/flora disturbance; dust and noise; health and safety risks; land acquisition and resettlement; forced displacement of IDPs; past land disputes; weak capacity to prevent adverse impacts; GBV and sexual exploitation risks; security risks for project workers.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared and disclosed; Sectoral Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (SESIA) to be conducted; Site-specific ESIAs and ESMPs; Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and Resettlement Action Plans where needed; Labor Management Procedures (LMP); GBV Mitigation Action Plan; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP); Dedicated E&S specialists in PIUs; E&S consulting firm for support; Owner's Engineer responsible for E&S aspects; Forced labor declarations required from bidders; Design alternatives to minimize land acquisition; Dust-proof cabins for sensitive equipment; Routine O&M including panel cleaning; Training on E&S standards

identified_risks.risk_description: Much of Somalia remains in conflict affecting access to project sites and insecurity for staff of government agencies and contractors. Concrete threat vectors require in-depth security risk assessments. SESRP will be implemented across diverse and contested geographical space.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Security Risk Management Framework prepared; Project-wide Security Management Plan (SMP) to be developed; Flexibility in site selection subject to security situation; Contingency plans for restricted site access; Standard operating procedures for contractors; Dedicated security advisor in each PIU; Government support and security arrangements; Provisional sum for contractor security costs

identified_risks.risk_description: Increased vaccination availability outlook will enable resumption of travel and reduce risk regarding commencement of construction works and delays in imports of construction materials. However, impacts on demand, nonpayment, and supply chains remain uncertain.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will address growing need to fight COVID-19 by making reliable electricity available to health institutions; BESS to reduce operational costs; Expected vaccination rollout to protect population

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Standards 1 (Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts)
Environmental and Social Standards 2 (Labor and Working Conditions)
Environmental and Social Standards 3 (Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management)
Environmental and Social Standards 4 (Community Health and Safety)
Environmental and Social Standards 5 (Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement)
Environmental and Social Standards 6 (Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources)
Environmental and Social Standards 10 (Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-wide Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established with multiple contact channels (phone, text, WhatsApp). GRM housed at MoEWR (FGS) and MoEM (Somaliland). Grievance Redress Committees (GRCs) at municipality/local government level composed of local leaders, municipal representatives, project staff, CBOs, legal aid, and law enforcement. Digital platform for tracking resolution. Separate mechanism for worker grievances. NGO GBV service provider to manage SEA/SH complaints. Project-affected persons can also use World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender gaps identified: 75% of women are illiterate; 44-66% of households are female-headed; women's labor force participation is 23.1% vs 76.3% for men; less than 10% of STEM graduates are women; women in energy sector mostly in administrative roles; cultural norms limit women's employment in technical fields; lack of equal opportunity policies. Project includes: Gender Action Plan with diagnostic assessment, pilot Women in Energy Incubator for training and placement, gender capacity building, prioritization of maternal health facilities, support for women's STEM education, policy and legal framework development, sex-disaggregated monitoring, women-only consultation sessions, focus on women-owned SMEs. Target: 20% increase in women's employment in energy sector by project end.
vulnerable_groups: Nomadic and semi-nomadic pastoralists (60% of population)
Rural populations (60% live in rural areas)
Women and girls (exposed to GBV, health risks from biomass fuel use, limited economic opportunities)
Persons with disabilities (implied in vulnerable groups)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Supports World Bank FCV Strategy 2020-25 by: (1) Preventing violent conflict by addressing drivers of fragility (economic/social exclusion, gender inequality, climate change); (2) Supporting transition out of fragility through renewed social contract and strengthened institutional legitimacy and capacity; (3) Fostering healthy local private sector and public-private solutions for jobs, services, social cohesion, inclusive development; (4) Mitigating FCV spillovers through regional approach. Project prioritizes electricity services to health and education facilities as COVID-19 recovery response and resilience building. Electricity provision seen as key to shoring up government legitimacy and reestablishing state authority. Project supports reestablishment of public institutions and sector governance. Emphasis on private sector (ESPs) participation while maintaining government oversight. Designed to build confidence in institutions and create visible benefits of state authority.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia is a fragile and conflict-affected state recovering from prolonged civil conflict. Key fragility factors: (1) Weak government capacity and nascent institutions; (2) Asymmetric federal structures with tensions between FGS, FMS, and Somaliland; (3) Low state legitimacy and limited public trust in government; (4) Security concerns with ongoing conflict in south and central regions; (5) Human capital deficits with 75% female illiteracy; (6) Extreme poverty (70% below poverty line); (7) Climate vulnerability (ranked 181/188 countries) with recurring droughts and floods affecting 5.2 million people; (8) Weak health system (maternal mortality 732/100,000 births); (9) Limited access to basic services including electricity (35% access rate); (10) Destroyed public infrastructure from conflict; (11) Ad-hoc service provision by private sector with limited regulation; (12) Lack of overarching regulations and codes of practice. Project addresses these by rebuilding sector institutions, establishing regulatory frameworks, improving service delivery, and creating economic opportunities.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is a major implementation challenge. Much of Somalia remains in conflict affecting site access and staff safety. Project includes: (1) Security Risk Management Framework; (2) Project-wide Security Management Plan (SMP); (3) Dedicated security advisor in each PIU; (4) Flexibility in site selection based on security situation; (5) Contingency plans for restricted access; (6) Standard operating procedures for contractors; (7) Government security arrangements and clearances; (8) Provisional sum for contractor security costs; (9) Allowance for permissible subcontracting to national contractors for local security management; (10) In-depth security risk assessments for each site. Project design offers flexibility to undertake activities in low-security-risk areas and select new sites if needed.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) are a significant concern. Forced displacement of IDPs is reported to be rampant in urban centers (Mogadishu, Hargeisa, Garowe), sometimes carried out in anticipation of project investment. Project includes: (1) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) to address land acquisition and resettlement; (2) Selection criteria for Component 3 facilities include presence of IDPs and high poverty levels; (3) Design alternatives to minimize land acquisition (underground/tunneling routing, poles with minimal footprints); (4) E&S screening to assess current land use, disputes, and claims; (5) Avoidance of physical or economic displacement where possible; (6) Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) in exceptional situations for settlement of past claims and compensation; (7) Government budget provision for land and resettlement compensation (noted as exceptional provision needed); (8) Challenges acknowledged due to gaps in legal/regulatory frameworks and land appropriation/asset valuation difficulties. Project aims to benefit IDPs through improved health and education services and economic opportunities.
